caveats: a: OEWS combines pipe, tube, wire, and rolled-shape manufacturing.
a: The technology evidence includes non-U.S. and integrated mills and does not measure adoption among LMM rerollers.
rho: Automatic control can improve quality without proportionate labor substitution.
rho: Small specialty rerollers and high-volume flat-product operations have different data and capital readiness.
e: Census reports 244 establishments, not firms or LMM candidates.
e: The frozen n uses a Machinery margin rather than observed reroller economics, making band assignment uncertain.
s5: The source is not steel-specific and does not measure completed transfers.
s5: Plant or product-line sales may not qualify as control transfers of eligible firms.
q: Plate, strip, rebar, merchant bar, rail, and specialty sections have different commercial dynamics.
q: CMC also melts steel and fabricates rebar, extending beyond the exact purchased-steel lens.
d5: BLS output covers all 331200 products rather than 331221 and is real value, not physical tons.
d5: Public infrastructure funding timing does not map one-for-one to rolled-shape shipments.
o: Imports can replace domestic operators without making software the supplier.
o: Operator requirement does not imply unchanged labor intensity.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat spot, program, and contract sales to construction, infrastructure, service-center, automotive, machinery, agricultural, energy, petrochemical, and general manufacturing customers, priced by ton, dimensions, grade, finish, processing, freight, and steel-market conditions.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive rolling lines, integrated steelmakers rolling their own steel, wire drawing, downstream fabrication, service-center distribution, passive entities, and firms without transferable operations.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. manufacturers repeatedly rolling or drawing plate, sheet, strip, rod, bar, rebar, light structural sections, rails, and other non-wire shapes from purchased steel for external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures all NAICS 331200 purchased-steel product manufacturing, not rolled shapes alone, and shows a workforce spanning production, maintenance, material moving, technical, management, sales, and office roles. Hitachi demonstrates AI control of cold rolling based on operator records and product shape data, while rolling-mill research demonstrates real-time machine vision. I translate these task and occupation sources into wage-weighted remaining exposure for 331221. Mature PLC and gauge control likely make gross technical capability overstate not-yet-automated opportunity.
caveats: OEWS combines pipe, tube, wire, and rolled-shape manufacturing.
The technology evidence includes non-U.S. and integrated mills and does not measure adoption among LMM rerollers.
a.change_evidence: A mill-level task-and-wage study separating current automatic gauge and shape control from manual inspection, adjustment, maintenance, material handling, scheduling, quality, sales, and office work would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist surface inspection, dimensional and shape control, roll-wear prediction, maintenance, furnace and line settings, scheduling, quality documentation, purchasing, quoting, and administration. Physical coil and billet handling, roll changes, maintenance, safety, metallurgy, and exception decisions remain difficult to substitute.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broader 331200 from $25.6 billion in 2024 to $30.9 billion in 2034, a 1.9% annual rate. CMC identifies infrastructure, manufacturing reshoring, energy generation and transmission, housing shortage, and data-center construction as structural demand supports, with 60% of IIJA funding remaining at the end of 2025. I use these broader output and end-market indicators for a five-year rolled-shape quantity ratio. They may overstate demand if integrated mills capture growth or steel intensity falls, and understate niche specialty-section growth.
caveats: BLS output covers all 331200 products rather than 331221 and is real value, not physical tons.
Public infrastructure funding timing does not map one-for-one to rolled-shape shipments.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year AISI or CRU shipment forecast for U.S. purchased-steel rerollers by plate, sheet, strip, rod, rebar, merchant bar, rail, and specialty shape in physical tons would materially improve the estimate.
d5.rationale: Construction, infrastructure, manufacturing, energy, data centers, and equipment support modest growth. Housing and rate sensitivity, imports, material substitution, project delays, inventory cycles, and integrated-mill competition create meaningful downside.

caveats: Census reports 244 establishments, not firms or LMM candidates.
The frozen n uses a Machinery margin rather than observed reroller economics, making band assignment uncertain.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level review of the 82 frozen candidates covering ultimate ownership, external revenue, normalized EBITDA through steel cycles, product and customer concentration, permits, plant status, and capital needs would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Most independent rerollers repeatedly supply external customers, but captive lines, subsidiaries of larger steel groups, firms outside the LMM band, distressed plants, customer concentration, environmental liabilities, and operations dependent on one product or contract reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 331221 ($1,891,212,938) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 331221 ($15,915,539,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $1,206,545 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 331221); class <5: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,317,658 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $258,524 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,306,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,041,037 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,615,182 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,494,099 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,619,255 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,361,898 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $134,460,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $26,381,117 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $310,592,424 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $60,938,234 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 82

caveats: Imports can replace domestic operators without making software the supplier.
Operator requirement does not imply unchanged labor intensity.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers broadly insourcing rolling, autonomous certified mills without accountable operating organizations, or rapid substitution away from steel shapes would lower the estimate.
o.rationale: Customers still require an accountable manufacturer to heat, roll or draw, control dimensions and metallurgy, inspect, certify, handle, and deliver physical steel shapes. AI can automate internal work but cannot replace the producing entity within five years.

q.bridge: Commercial Metals reports that many rebar and merchant-bar end markets are construction-driven, evaluates sales through price per ton and tons shipped, fills steel orders from inventory or near-completion, and describes competitive bidding in downstream rebar. I translate this large integrated producer's pricing environment to LMM purchased-steel rerollers. Scale may understate niche flexibility but integration may overstate retained margins; transparent steel costs and bids generally reduce retention.
caveats: Plate, strip, rebar, merchant bar, rail, and specialty sections have different commercial dynamics.
CMC also melts steel and fabricates rebar, extending beyond the exact purchased-steel lens.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm contracts, invoices, surcharge formulas, bid tabs, price-reset frequency, product premiums, and post-automation margin persistence would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Yield, uptime, energy, tolerance, surface quality, and specialty-section improvements can be partly retained, but steel input prices, short order cycles, bids, service-center comparisons, imports, and customer negotiations share visible savings over five years.

caveats: Automatic control can improve quality without proportionate labor substitution.
Small specialty rerollers and high-volume flat-product operations have different data and capital readiness.
rho.change_evidence: Deployment cohorts from U.S. rolling mills reporting commissioning time, shape and defect performance, operator overrides, uptime, false rejects, maintenance outcomes, and realized labor hours saved would narrow the range.
rho.rationale: Continuous process data, cameras, gauges, vibration sensors, and established control systems support implementation. Legacy equipment, high speeds and temperatures, safety validation, product changeovers, scarce defect labels, cybersecurity, integration downtime, and operator trust limit full five-year conversion.

s5.bridge: SBA reports that 47% of family-business owners expecting retirement within five years lack a successor. I use this as broad succession evidence, then haircut because it is cross-industry and because no successor can mean closure, family resolution, employee ownership, asset sale, or continued operation rather than a qualifying control transfer. Capital intensity and strategic ownership likely make the raw figure overstate s5.
caveats: The source is not steel-specific and does not measure completed transfers.
Plant or product-line sales may not qualify as control transfers of eligible firms.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and AIST transaction records mapped to eligible 331221 firms, plus beneficial-owner age and succession interviews, would replace the broad proxy.
s5.rationale: Independent rerollers may be founder- or family-owned, but environmental diligence, equipment condition, union obligations, commodity-cycle timing, and substantial working capital can delay or prevent going-concern sales.

principal_driver: Sensor-rich rolling processes offer implementable AI improvements in inspection, shape control, uptime, and scheduling.
principal_weakness: Transparent steel pricing and mature automation limit both remaining labor substitution and five-year retained benefit.
